sections.title: Fats
sections.narration: 0.7g of fat. saturated fat, cholesterol, and polyunsaturated fat are doing most of the work. for meats, fat quality matters a lot once the protein is already there.

sections.title: Proteins
sections.narration: 17.8g of protein. bioavailability and amino acid quality are both strong. strong protein is expected here, so the rest of the profile decides how high it climbs.

sections.title: Vitamins
sections.narration: vitamin b12 at 54% daily value, vitamin d at 8% daily value stand out most. that is only mild support overall.

sections.narration: minerals are basically not part of the case here.

sections.title: Pros
sections.narration: on the plus side: high satiety protein source because that is a major real-world strength for meat. complete protein delivery is reliable because the category is supposed to do this well. easy to centre a meal around because practical meal value matters.

sections.title: Cons
sections.narration: the drawbacks next: fat quality can go sideways fast because some meats are strong until the fat profile shows up. cost and storage can be a pain because practical downsides still count. brings no fibre support because that leaves a nutrition gap the rest of the meal has to cover.

closing.summary: Cod has a genuinely strong overall case. the practical downsides are real, but they do not outweigh the upside. best when you want efficient protein and can accept the category tradeoffs that come with it.
